title: Sql ('Q')
html:
Sql ('Q')

  • Last UpdatedOkt 15, 2024
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

Submit a DML statement to the DBMS.

Description

Table 1. Modes
Mode Description
Mode 0 Send a statement in the native Data Manipulation Language (DML) to the DBMS. If the statement selects data from the database, pass the first field of the last row of the result table to Uniface.
Mode 1 Reserved for future use. Return 0 to Uniface.
Mode 2 Send a statement in the native DML to the DBMS. If the statement selects data from the database (for example, select in SQL), format the result table according to a Uniface convention. Pass the first set of result rows to Uniface.
Mode 3 Pass a subsequent set of result rows to Uniface.

From Uniface

Preconditions realized by Uniface—If the field uinfo->mode is set to a number less than or equal to seven in the Info call, the connector is logged on to the DBMS.

Table 2. Information Available from Uniface
Data Structure->Element Description Modes
uctrl->iobuf Points to the general I/O buffer. All
uctrl->sqlbuf Points to the SQL buffer. All
uctrl->TMEnabled Indicates whether the connector is TM-controlled. All
uctrl->udrvini Unique identification of the current logon path. All
uctrl->udrvnum Connector sequence number. All
uctrl->uilist Points to the index list. All
uctrl->ulock Lock indicator. All
uctrl->umisc Points to the null-terminated DML string to send to the DBMS. Mode 0 and 2

To Uniface

Table 3. Modes
Mode Description
Mode 0 If data is selected from the database, pass the first field of the last selected row back to Uniface in the general I/O buffer as a null-terminated string. If no row is selected, the general I/O buffer must be cleared by setting the first character to NULL.
Mode 2 There are two ways to handle the mode 2 select statement. One method allows the mode 2 to be completed in a single step, (that is without Sql mode 3 calls) when the function uaddsql is used to flush the buffer.

In the second method, tthe result table must be formatted according to the following conventions:

  • The width of every column equals the maximum length of the field.
  • Columns are separated by a space.
  • Values in the columns are left-aligned.
  • Every field in the first row contains the field name or the name of the aggregate function for that field in the result table (names are left-aligned).
  • Every field in the second row contains the equal sign (=) to underline the ‘titles’.
  • There can be zero or more result rows.
The string representation for the native missing value symbol of the DBMS (the NULL value) is the empty string. For example:
FIELD1 FIELD2 FIELD3
====== ====== ======
VALUE1 DATA1 STRING1
VALUE2 MORE_DATA2
VALUE3 OTHER_DATA3OTHER_STRING3

Every row in the result table must be terminated by a carriage return character. The entire string that is stored in the general I/O buffer with one call to the connector Sql request must be null-terminated. The rows of the result table must be stored in the general I/O buffer, up to the maximum length of eight kilobytes. If more rows are available, set uctrl->umode to 3. Uniface can then call Sql mode 3 to fetch the remaining rows.
Mode 3 Store a subsequent set of result rows in the general I/O buffer, up to the maximum length of eight kilobytes. The rules for formatting, line and string termination are the same as for mode 2. Do not repeat the header lines.

Set uctrl->umode to 0 to indicate that the last row has been fetched.

When the connector request return value is less than 0, an error code must be supplied in uctrl->udrverr.
Table 4. Connector Request Return Values
Value Description
>0 The number of hits selected in Sql mode 0, if the statements selected data from the database.
0 SQL was successfully executed.
Note: An exception here is when an SQL select statement was issued in Mode 0. For example, an sql ProcScript select command. In this case, the number of selected rows must be returned.
-3 Exceptional error, or DBMS reported an SQL error.
-9 I/O server logon error.
-11 Exceptional error, or DBMS reported an SQL error.
-16 DBMS reported network error.

Extra information

The Sql request is provided with a dummy control block. This control block does not describe a table and it does not have an associated connector workspace.

If the DBMS does not support a query language, return -3 to Uniface.

This request is called Sql because most of the supported query languages are SQL-like. Any query language can be supported, however. The SQL query was either entered by the end user in the Uniface SQL Workbench (mode 2 and 3), or generated with the ProcScript statement sql (mode 0). Uniface has no knowledge of the contents of the query and therefore does not provide the connector with information about the lengths and types of the fields in the result table. The values in the result table must be presented to Uniface as strings. If the DBMS is not capable of performing the required formatting, the connector must take care of this.

Uniface can cancel a mode 2 to mode 3 sequence without notifying the connector.

TM-Controlled Connectors

TM-controlled connectors are not allowed to begin, commit or rollback transactions.

Connectors that are TM-controlled do not receive Commit/Rollback requests. Therefore, connectors that normally perform cursor administration at the end of a transaction may not be able to determine the state of a cursor when the connector is TM-controlled.

The following is recommended for TM-controlled DBMS connectors:

  • In SQL mode 2, do not reuse a cursor from a previous SQL Workbench request. Instead, close the cursor and reopen it. It is possible that an error may occur if the cursor was implicitly closed by the DBMS at the end of a transaction. In this case, the connector can choose to ignore DBMS errors which indicate that the cursor is already closed.
  • Close the cursor after all data has been returned for a SQL Workbench request.
Related Links
  • uctrl

    Exchanges general information between Uniface and the connector, and describes a table.

  • uinfo

    Supplies Uniface with basic information about the DBMS and the connector.

  • uaddsql

    Flushes the SQL buffer or the I/O buffer, depending on the mode of the connector request.

  • TM-Controlled DBMS Connectors

    A database connector can be controlled by a transaction manager only if it is TM-capable. Uniface does not provide TM-controlled connectors and does not directly support third-party transaction managers. However, you can use the APIs provided by the Database Connector Interface to build a custom database connector that does.

Was this topic helpful?
LikeDislike
